title: Tamil Medical and Shaiva Manuscripts
focus: Detailed look at the IAS Tamil Medical Manuscript Collection and the Shaiva Manuscripts of Pondicherry, focusing on their content and historical significance.
rationale: Fallback section derived from packet-level grouping.
sectionExcerpt:
[Page 015]
INDIA'S SUBMISSIONS TO MEMORY OF THE WORLD REGISTERS
IAS Tamil Medical Manuscript Collection (Successfully inscribed in 1997)
- A collection of 564 palm-leaf manuscripts, mostly from the 19th century, documenting Siddha and Ayurveda medical traditions.
- This system explains the methods of obtaining medicines from herbs, herbal roots, leaves, flowers, barks, fruits etc. The proportions of the ingredients as well as the specific processes are explained in detail.

[Page 016]
Śaiva Manuscripts of Pondicherry (Successfully inscribed in 2005)
• The largest collection of Śaiva Siddhanta texts, consisting of 11,000 manuscripts on Shaivism, Vishnu worship, astrology, medicine, and literature.
• In the 10th century CE, this religious tradition, a major current of Hinduism, was spread right across the Indian subcontinent and beyond, as far as Cambodia in the East.
• It represented the mainstream of Tantric doctrine and Shaiva worship.
• Its surviving texts, the majority of them unpublished, range from the 6th century CE to the colonial period.
